Here is a detailed summary of Chapter 4: How to Mean the Body of Christ? Pico’s Q6 from Pico della Mirandola on Trial: Heresy, Freedom, and Philosophy by Brian P. Copenhaver: 📘 Chapter 4: How to Mean the Body of Christ? Pico’s Q6 This chapter is devoted to Pico’s treatment of Question 6 (Q6) in his Apology , which addresses one of the most theologically delicate and metaphysically complex topics in Christianity: the Eucharist . Specifically, it focuses on the real presence of Christ’s body in the consecrated bread and wine of the Mass, a doctrine that had long been a site of controversy, precision, and peril. Copenhaver shows that Pico’s approach in Q6 is a model of scholastic philosophical theology : a dense, exacting argument that draws on theories of metaphysics , semantics , and sacramental theology , particularly concerning the words of consecration—“This is my body.” 🍞 4.1 The Eucharistic Context The key dogma in question is transubstantiation : Defined at the Fourth Lateran Council (1215) and reaffirmed at Trent , it holds that the substance of the bread becomes the substance of Christ’s body, while the accidents (taste, smell, appearance) remain. This doctrine relies heavily on Aristotelian substance theory , particularly as interpreted by Aquinas. The challenge Pico faces: How can the words “This is my body” be true if the bread remains in any form? 🧩 4.2 Pico’s Controversial Thesis One of Pico’s 900 Conclusions stated: “The body of Christ is not really and truly in the sacrament.” This is shocking on its face —a direct challenge to the Church’s teaching. But Pico insists in Q6 of the Apology that: His thesis was misread . What he meant is not that Christ is absent from the sacrament, but that the word “body” in the sentence “This is my body” does not have real or true supposition —that is, it does not refer to a physically present body in the way ordinary speech would imply. 🧠 4.3 Supposition Theory and Eucharistic Language Copenhaver shows that Pico’s defense turns on the medieval theory of supposition : Suppositio materialis : when a word refers to itself as a word. Suppositio formalis : when a word stands for a concept. Suppositio personalis : when a word stands for the actual thing it signifies. In “This is my body,” Pico argues that the term “body” does not have personal supposition —it does not refer to the physical body of Christ as a spatial object —because: The physical body of Christ is not present locally on the altar. Rather, it is present sacramentally , by virtue of a divine institution , not by ordinary reference. 🧵 In short: The sentence “This is my body” is true, but not in the ordinary sense of language . It is true sacramentally , not semantically. 📚 4.4 Pico’s Sources and Logic Copenhaver traces Pico’s logic to Jean Cabrol and Pierre de la Palud , both of whom had proposed subtle alternatives to the standard Thomist interpretation of transubstantiation. These thinkers tried to explain the real presence of Christ in ways that preserved orthodoxy while avoiding certain metaphysical absurdities , such as the annihilation of the bread's substance. Pico draws on their ideas to introduce the concept of sustaining breadness ( substentatio paneitatis ), where the appearance of bread remains , not as a deception, but as a kind of sign or support . This leads Pico to propose: The truth of the sacramental statement lies not in the referential structure of the sentence, but in its sacramental efficacy —what it brings about. ✝️ 4.5 Real Presence vs. Real Reference Copenhaver clarifies Pico’s strategy: He is not denying the real presence of Christ in the sacrament . He is denying that the sentence “This is my body” functions like an ordinary descriptive sentence. It is more like a performative utterance —a statement that accomplishes what it says , not merely describes it. Pico, then, anticipates a quasi-Austinian view of sacramental language: the truth of the Eucharist lies in the performative act , not in conventional semantics. 💡 This makes the sacrament unique in the structure of language: it is a theological event rather than a normal linguistic proposition. 🧱 4.6 Philosophical Stakes Copenhaver emphasizes that Pico’s argument has deep philosophical consequences : It challenges the naive realism of sacramental literalism . It insists that meaning is context-sensitive , especially when the speaker is divine . It preserves orthodoxy while rejecting mechanical readings of consecration. Pico also warns against semantic idolatry —treating sacred language as though its surface meaning exhausts its divine function . 📖 4.7 Opposition and Danger Despite his careful distinctions, Pico’s position was politically and theologically dangerous : The papal commission condemned his thesis. His explanation required deep familiarity with scholastic logic , which his judges lacked. His tone in the Apology —mocking and adversarial—did not help his cause. Still, Copenhaver argues, Pico was not attempting to undermine the Eucharist but to defend it with philosophical rigor . ✅ Chapter Takeaway In Q6, Pico confronts the doctrine of transubstantiation not with denial but with philosophical precision : He affirms the real presence of Christ in the sacrament. He denies that the sentence “This is my body” has true supposition in the ordinary sense. He appeals to a rich tradition of scholastic semantics to argue that sacramental truth is not reducible to semantic truth . He insists that words in the liturgy function differently —their power is institutional and divine , not grammatical. Copenhaver reads Pico here as a bold scholastic , not a modern critic. His theology is not progressive, but technically orthodox , and his danger lies in being too precise for his own safety .
